_first_class_status: Reference implementation — the tradition Forge ships with the deepest QC rigor. New traditions inherit this file's shape but tune the specifics.

origin.antiquity: Documented since the Ramayana era; modern commercial form since 1934 Bihar earthquake exposed home wall paintings to outside attention
origin.primary_practitioners: Women artists of Mithila — historically anonymous; contemporary recognition includes Sita Devi, Ganga Devi, Mahasundari Devi, Bharti Dayal, and the Kohbar Ghar tradition
origin.geocultural_landscape: Brahmin (kachni line-work), Kayastha (bharni color-fill), and Dusadh (godna tattoo-like) sub-styles each carry distinct grammars

visual_signature.line_discipline: Bold double-contour hand-drawn keylines with weight variation; never airbrushed or vector-perfect
visual_signature.color_strategy: Flat folk color zones; saturated indigo, vermillion, saffron, leaf-green, ochre; NO photographic gradients
visual_signature.composition: Flat silhouette; no perspective; figures fill the frame; dense interior decoration with 5-9 distinct zones per subject
visual_signature.ornament_density: Ornate — every interior surface decorated with motifs (lotus, fish, peacock-eye, paisley, dots, leaf veins)
visual_signature.background_strategy: Filled with secondary motifs (vine, dots, geometric borders); NEVER blank white space
visual_signature.negative_space_treatment: 8-12 small ornamental flourishes in any negative areas; the canvas is never empty
visual_signature.ground_line: Dotted Mithila ground line beneath the subject (lotus, peepal leaf, fish, or dotted earth)

prompt_scaffold.positive_prefix: Madhubani folk-art icon of {subject}, flat silhouette, bold double-contour hand-drawn keylines, saturated folk-color body fill, dense interior decoration with 5-9 distinct ornament zones, dotted Mithila ground line, ornate borders with leaf-and-flower chain, traditional Mithila palette of indigo + vermillion + saffron + leaf-green
prompt_scaffold.negative_prefix: photorealistic, 3D render, fur texture, photographic shading, perspective depth, blank white background, cartoonish big eyes, airbrushed gradients, watercolor wash, oil-paint texture, modern flat-design illustration, vector minimalism, naturalistic animal photography, sketch outline only
hard_negatives_v3: no anatomically uncanny faces (almond eyes only — never round cartoon eyes)
the body interior MUST be decorated — never plain color
stripes must read as stripes (vertical bands), not floral medallions
subject must remain anatomically identifiable as its species
double-contour outlines must be hand-drawn-style, not vector-clean
ornament zones must be COUNTED (5-9 distinct), not infinite
color must be flat folk-color, not photo-real
ground line must be present (dotted, lotus, peepal, or fish ground)
if a serpent is depicted, hood proportional and no exaggerated forked tongue

pilot_results.outcome: Visible style transfer from photoreal to ink-line folk-art between step 0 and step 50; gallery at docs/gallery/lora_pilot/

production_recipe.caption_template: a madhubani folk-art painting of [SUBJECT], [POSE], traditional mithila tradition, flat folk colors, ornate decoration zones

icon_64.reason: Ornate motif density loses legibility under 256px

icon_128.reason: Same — recommend Warli or minimal-animal for app-icon needs

sticker_256.notes: Bold subjects (tiger, peacock) read; small-feature subjects (mongoose, kingfisher) lose detail

cultural_attribution.tradition_owners: Women artists of the Mithila region, Bihar, India
cultural_attribution.mandatory_attribution_text: This Madhubani-tradition artifact draws on the folk-art lineage of the Mithila region, Bihar, India. Originated and sustained by women artists of the Mithila tradition.
cultural_attribution.commercial_use_note: Commercial use should support cultural-preservation organizations such as the Mithila Art Institute (Madhubani), Crafts Council of India, or direct Mithila-artist cooperatives.
cultural_attribution.reference_corpus_license_summary: All references CC-BY-SA or public domain via Wikimedia Commons; per-asset attribution.json receipts in references/

novelty_handling.policy: Subjects newly introduced to the Madhubani tradition (e.g., snow leopards, dugongs, hoolock gibbons — animals not historically depicted in Mithila paintings) are explicitly disclosed as contemporary interpolations in the receipt's `cultural_attribution.novelty_disclosure` field.
novelty_handling.default_disclosure_template: {subject} is not traditionally depicted in Madhubani; this rendering is a contemporary interpolation honoring the tradition's grammar (flat silhouette, dense interior decoration, traditional palette) while extending its subject vocabulary to include species native to {region}.
